data.title: Self-made China milk tea entrepreneur marries wealthy solar heiress, captures public attention
data.description:
The upcoming wedding of the founder of a renowned milk tea company from a grass-roots background and the daughter of a wealthy family that owns a leading solar energy firm has captivated the Chinese public and is trending online.
Zhang Junjie, the driving force behind the Chinese milk tea brand Chagee, and his bride Gao Haichun, whose parents are the founders of Trina Solar, will celebrate their nuptials at the Zhongwu Hotel in Changzhou, eastern Jiangsu province, on December 15, according to Jiemian News.
Both Zhang and Gao are 32 years old.
Gao, a Brown University graduate, has been hailed as the most beautiful second-generation figure in China’s solar energy sector, often referred to as the “Solar Goddess.” She also holds an executive position at her parents’ company and is their only child.

Trina Solar is publicly listed in both New York and Shanghai.
What has particularly fascinated internet users is Zhang’s inspiring journey from humble beginnings.
Born into poverty in Kunming, Yunnan, southwest China, Zhang became an orphan at the age of 10. Information regarding his parents’ deaths and details about any relatives remain undisclosed.
With minimal formal education, Zhang began working as an apprentice at a milk tea store in Kunming at 17.
He learned to read and write at 18. At that store, he advanced from manager’s assistant to manager, and then regional supervisor. Within five years, he became an agent of that milk tea brand and a franchise partner.

Zhang also had a brief tenure from 2015 to 2017 at a Shanghai-based robotics company, where he handled external cooperation.
In November 2017, Zhang launched Chagee, a milk tea brand inspired by traditional Chinese aesthetics.
While many of his peers focused on fruit milk tea, Zhang distinguished himself by prioritising fresh tea leaves as his primary ingredient.
He expressed to the media his ambition to rival Starbucks with his Chinese tea offerings.
With 6,000 stores both in China and abroad, Chagee was officially listed on NASDAQ in April.

Many of Zhang’s employees admire his unwavering dedication to his career.
“To this day, he hasn’t bought a car or a house. He lacks materialistic desires and aspires to leave a mark in history,” an anonymous employee acquainted with Zhang shared with Jiemian News.
Zhang and Gao first crossed paths at a networking event for young entrepreneurs, and they registered their marriage in June.
On November 21, Zhang addressed a rumour circulating online—that he had previously married a tycoon’s disabled daughter and had a child with her—by releasing a statement on social media.
This unfounded rumour emerged late last year, leading a Shanghai court to mandate a public apology to Zhang from the individual responsible earlier this year.
Despite this clarification, many internet users continued to discuss the rumour following reports of Zhang’s wedding in mid-November.

“I feel it’s essential to speak out openly. I want to clarify that I have never been married before meeting Haichun. I aim to protect my wife and family from any defamation stemming from this rumour,” Zhang stated on social media.
The couple’s impending marriage has sparked considerable public interest on mainland social media, going viral with 87 million views on a leading platform.
“He started from scratch. Now, he’s marrying a beautiful and wealthy woman. He’s someone every man envies,” commented one online observer.
“He has quickly risen in the business realm. He is undoubtedly a genius!” praised another.
A third user remarked: “It seems as if the inspiring hero from a novel has come to life.”

data.title: Deadly boat accident cover-up reveals corruption in China’s fishing industry
data.description:
A local government official in northeast China has been accused of covering up a fishing boat accident and preventing the vessel’s owner from calling the police for help, and investigations sparked by the incident have exposed a series of corruption cases related to fishing industry safety.
The accident took place on November 10. A boat registered in Yingkou, Liaoning province, capsized while crew members were fishing, causing 11 to fall into the water. Two were rescued and nine have been reported missing, according to the Liaoning Daily on Wednesday.
Official investigations found corruption in the approval process and arrangements for the fishing trip.
The boat owner, surnamed Zheng, used other people’s qualifications to get past safety checks. During the trip, he arranged for two people who lacked valid crew certificates to board the boat. This meant the fishing vessel failed to meet requirements for offshore operations, resulting in serious safety risks, the report said. Zheng was detained by local police on November 16.
The party secretary of a local district fisheries and marine bureau not only delayed rescue but also had a staff member convince the boat owner not to call the police in an attempt to cover up the truth. The report said the official and staff member, surnamed Xu and Chi, respectively, were awaiting trial.
On November 11, the Chinese embassy in South Korea said in a statement that two fishing vessels had capsized in waters near the country in the two days prior, leaving two dead and 12 missing. One of the incidents the embassy described matched the details in the Yingkou report.
On November 10, the Liaoning provincial government held a video conference highlighting the importance of fishing safety, demanding local governments adopt a “highly responsible attitude and take decisive, forceful measures to curb the frequent occurrence of accidents”, according to the report.


Local governments were also asked to investigate the causes of these incidents.
“Are there instances of partially deploying central government policies? Is there a tendency for local officials to abandon their responsibilities? Is there a corruption problem? Is there a pattern for empty slogans and poor execution?” the provincial authorities asked, according to the report.
The campaign is focused on key links in the fishing industry, including vessel inspection, fishing port supervision, crew certification and port transfer approval. It targets law enforcement personnel, vessel owners and captains and fishing port operators.
As of Monday, disciplinary authorities at all levels in the province had filed cases against 162 people, and police had detained 104 and solved 45 cases. Another 52 people had turned themselves in to disciplinary authorities, the report said.
Besides corruption, authorities found other illegal practices in the cases. They include buying boat certificates from owners and selling them to people without the legal document, who in turn bribed officials in charge of approving fishing trips to change the database and give the boats legal status.
One official who turned himself in told authorities he had accepted four boxes of seafood and two cartons of cigarettes to ignore illegal fishing operations.
“I know they wanted to maintain a good relationship with me so that I would turn a blind eye to their lack of safety equipment or qualified crew on their boats,” he said.
Investigations have found that those who took bribes were more lenient towards boats that were overstaffed, had inadequate firefighting equipment or sneaked out during the night, according to the report.
Following repeated calls from President Xi Jinping to strengthen safety practices, the 14th Five-Year National Work Safety Plan, published in 2022, vowed to “strengthen safety oversight of high-risk fishing vessels and fishing port waters and rigorously crack down on practices such as overloading, operating beyond safe wind limits or designated zones, and illegal activities involving unregistered, unlicensed or unregulated fishing vessels”.
To address regulatory loopholes, local governments in Liaoning have established supervision stations and upgraded an intelligent supervision system for fishing vessels with real-time monitoring of vessel departure and arrival reports, sailing routes and other critical data. Public hotlines are also open for the public to report violations and other concerns 24 hours a day, according to the report.

data.title: Thai flood victim bitten by deadly snake waits 32 hours for rescue
data.description:
A Thai mother bitten by a deadly snake endured a desperate 32-hour wait for rescue after being trapped by floods that have devastated southern Thailand for more than a week.
The woman, stranded in her submerged Hat Yai home, turned to social media at 3am on Wednesday after 24 hours of excruciating pain. She and her boyfriend had spent the previous day calling at least 10 rescue agencies, but floodwaters kept emergency crews from reaching them, according to the Thai Examiner news site.
Her plea quickly went viral, triggering an outpouring of support and fury online. Thousands urged authorities to save her as she described her bleeding gums, swollen legs and laboured breathing.
Yet she forced herself to smile, she wrote, as she tried to stay calm for the sake of her young child.
About eight hours after her first post, rescue teams finally reached her home. She was rushed to Songkhla Hospital where doctors administered life-saving antivenom and began monitoring her condition. In a later update, she thanked her online supporters for keeping her spirits alive.
Numerous snakes have been spotted swimming through the flooded streets of southern Thailand in recent days – flushed from their habitats by the relentless rain. A viral video posted on Tuesday showed a massive serpent gliding through muddy waters, adding to the sense of unease among those still awaiting evacuation.

น้ำท่วมก็หนักหนาแล้ว ยังมาเจองูตัวเบ้อเริ่ม ไม่รู้ว่าจงอาจหรือเปล่า!?#น้ำท่วมหาดใหญ่ pic.twitter.com/ll5vj0LPST
— joe black (@joe_black317) November 25, 2025

The floods, described by officials as the worst in decades, have so far killed more than 80 people and affected over 3 million households across 12 southern provinces.
While waters receded in some areas on Thursday, levels remained dangerously high in Pattani and Nakhon Si Thammarat. In Songkhla province alone, more than 100 bodies have been recovered.
Songkhla Nakarin Hospital, designated as the area’s sole collection point for human remains, is working to identify the victims, according to The Nation newspaper.
Amid the devastation, moments of resilience have emerged. In one instance, a baby was born in the back of a 10-wheel truck after its driver helped three pregnant women trapped by traffic in Hat Yai on Thursday. The truck had been carrying flood evacuees from Rattaphum district to Songkhla city, some 90km (56 miles) away, the Thai Rath newspaper reported.

In Hat Yai, one of the hardest-hit cities, authorities are mobilising a massive clean-up operation.
The Songkhla provincial government has deployed an army of workers and machines: 100 trucks, 100 backhoes, 10 garbage vehicles, more than 10 million black bags and 300,000 cleaning kits.
More than 1,000 local residents have been hired at a daily wage of 400 baht (US$1.24) to help restore the city of 400,000, according to Thai Rath.

data.title: International MMA body formed to unify amateur governance, target Olympic inclusion
data.description:
A unifying international governing federation for mixed martial arts (MMA) will be launched on December 1, senior officials announced on Thursday at a press briefing. The new body aims to bring greater unity to the sport’s amateur structures and boost its case for Olympic inclusion.
The Federation of Mixed Martial Arts (Fima) was created in response to calls from athletes and national federations for a single cohesive body, said Gordon Tang, president of the Asian Mixed Martial Arts Association (AMMA). Tang is a founding partner and major backer of Fima.
“We are ready to collaborate with any organisation that shares a commitment to protecting and promoting the interests of MMA and its athletes,” he said.
At present, different international bodies such as the Global Association of Mixed Martial Arts (Gamma) and the International Mixed Martial Arts Federation (IMMAF) run their own world and continental championships, set rule frameworks for amateur competitions and work with national federations at grass-roots level.
Conversations with these bodies are ongoing, with the intention of building a collaborative framework.

“Our aim is to unify everybody so that we can put up a united front to the IOC,” said AMMA director general Galastein Tan.
Officials confirmed that early discussions with the IOC had taken place and described the response as positive. Tang said that while the timeline for Olympic recognition rested with the IOC and future host committees, Fima would “work very hard” to advance the process.
To gain Olympic recognition, Fima officials said they would have to show the IOC that the sport had a unified global governance model and would work with Olympic committees at a national level, improve athlete safety standards and expand global participation.
Tan said the federation’s vision drew from developments in Asia, where MMA was recently included in the Asian Youth Games and would appear in both the Asian Games and the Asian Indoor and Martial Arts Games in 2026.
He described this progress as an important step towards meeting the criteria required for eventual Olympic inclusion.
In its bid to increase safety standards, Tan said, Fima would adopt AMMA’s ruleset, which removes the cage and prohibits certain high-risk techniques such as head strikes on grounded opponents. He believed this would maintain the appeal of MMA while meeting safety expectations for multi-sport environments.
Regarding Olympic-style tournament formats, Tan said AMMA currently held three-day events with one bout per athlete per day and would continue refining scheduling with its technical and medical committees to ensure proper recovery time.
Officials said they expected the creation of national federations to be essential, particularly in countries where MMA was still governed under another combat sport body. Tan said that once a sport was included in major multi-sport events, national federations often received government support, helping to develop grass-roots participation.

data.title: Surge in Hong Kong dollar bonds to continue after record US$42 billion year
data.description:
Hong Kong dollar bond issuance is poised to expand, following a record run of offerings, as easing local interest rates attract more issuers while a global diversification trend persists, according to analysts.
Total issuance in the city’s currency reached a record HK$331 billion (US$42.6 billion) so far this year, nearly 37 per cent higher than 2024’s full-year total of HK$242 billion.
“The Hong Kong dollar bond market will continue to grow over the long term, supported by structural shifts,” said Oliver Greer, global head of medium-term notes at Standard Chartered.
Amid a global shift to diversify away from the US dollar, the Hong Kong dollar emerged as a logical choice for Asian investors, supported by its long-standing US dollar peg and a deep, liquid market underpinned by a clear legal and capital framework, Greer said. The city’s low inflation also translated into higher real returns for bond investors, he added.
Many issuers were broadening their funding mix as US dollar rates remained elevated, said Terrence Pang, portfolio manager at Fidelity International.
“The Hong Kong dollar has offered more competitive all-in costs, particularly for borrowers with natural Hong Kong dollar needs or balance-sheet alignment,” he said.
The Hong Kong dollar bond market has expanded steadily in recent years, but the latest surge was driven by the exceptionally low Hong Kong interbank offered rate (Hibor) earlier this year, which sharply reduced funding costs.
Although the rate gap between Hong Kong dollars and US dollars narrowed recently, Hong Kong dollar interest rates were still likely to stay below US dollar levels because of the city’s lower inflation, Greer said.
“We forecast a continued decline in Hibor throughout 2026, in tandem with the Federal Reserve’s aggressive rate-cutting trajectory,” said Carlos Casanova, an economist at Swiss private bank UBP. “We project a reduction of at least 100 basis points.”
Additionally, the Hong Kong dollar public bond market saw its first international issuer this year, issuing a so-called wonton bond, named after a type of Chinese dumpling.

Foreign entities issuing public bonds in Hong Kong dollars is a departure from their typical practice of using private channels to tap the local currency. The Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank set the precedent by issuing a HK$4 billion three-year bond in February.
The transaction list grew to eight this year, with Korea Expressway raising a HK$2 billion note last week as the latest and first corporate wonton-bond issuer.
“After years of virtually no international public Hong Kong dollar deals, one transaction quickly led to seven more in a year,” Greer said. “The market has matured to a point where international issuers choose to come here because pricing is more attractive and the size is significant.”
Issuance of wonton bonds this year totalled HK$28.9 billion. It could grow further next year as more supranationals and agencies debut or revisit the market, according to analysts.
“Many supranationals and agencies now see this as a long-term market where they plan to be repeat borrowers,” said Greer. “In 2026, we will likely see a number of debut international issuers doing Hong Kong dollar public deals, and the pipeline looks healthy.”
The outlook for Hong Kong dollar bonds next year was “constructive”, supported by fiscal and policy measures that continue to drive funding needs from government, quasi-sovereign and other high-grade local issuers, Pang said, pointing to infrastructure initiatives like the Northern Metropolis.
Local institutional investors tended to be “sticky” too, he added.
“Banks, insurers, [Mandatory Provident Fund] schemes and other long-term buy-and-hold types of investors provided support in the primary and secondary markets,” he said. “Loan-to-deposit ratios have fallen meaningfully since 2022, increasing banks’ and insurers’ appetite for high-quality carry assets.”
As mainland Chinese banks’ offshore balance sheets grew, Hong Kong became a natural funding centre and reinforced the case for allocating a larger share to Hong Kong dollars, Greer said.
Overall, the structural backdrop, including a rate-cutting cycle and tight credit spreads – an indicator that investors see low risk and demand little extra yield over safer government bonds – was expected to support Hong Kong dollar bond issuance, Greer said.
“If this supportive risk sentiment persists and the Fed delivers the expected rate cuts, 2026 is likely to be another strong year for Hong Kong dollar bond issuance,” he said.

data.title: Hong Kong’s Tai Po fire: what you need to know about city’s deadliest blaze in decades
data.description:
We have put together seven stories on everything you need to know about Hong Kong’s worst fire in decades. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. Hong Kong families endure unimaginable loss as fire death toll rises
Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades has torn apart families as the death toll increased early Friday morning, with many heavy-hearted relatives continuing to search for missing loved ones.
2. ‘Hongkongers are capable’: deadliest fire in decades spurs widespread aid efforts

People from all walks of life in Hong Kong rallied in support of victims of the city’s deadliest fire in decades, with aid ranging from millions in cash donations by major business groups to grass roots efforts by civilians delivering essential supplies.
3. Want to donate in cash or kind? How to help Hong Kong’s embattled fire victims
In the wake of the fire at Wang Fuk Court that devastated seven blocks of the estate in Tai Po on Wednesday, the community swiftly rallied to support displaced families, with organisations establishing collection points for essential supplies. Here’s where you can offer your donations – in cash or kind – to aid the relief effort.
4. ‘My superhero’: girlfriend mourns loss of firefighter killed in Hong Kong blaze

Pride mingled with grief as the family and loved ones of the Hong Kong firefighter who died while battling Wednesday’s disastrous high-rise inferno shared their feelings of tragic loss.
5. Hong Kong contractor for fire-hit Tai Po project has record of safety offences

The contractor behind renovation work at the site of Hong Kong’s worst fire in decades had previously breached safety requirements for construction projects on multiple occasions, the Post has found.
6. Hong Kong fire probe centres on widespread use of flammable material by building firm
The investigation into a deadly fire at a Hong Kong housing estate has centred on the use of flammable material in the scaffolding that covered the residential blocks, officials said on Thursday.
7. Should Hong Kong abandon bamboo scaffolding after Tai Po fire tragedy?

A deadly blaze that ravaged a four-decade-old public housing estate in Tai Po is once again casting a spotlight on one of Hong Kong’s most iconic yet contentious construction features: bamboo scaffolding.

data.title: Bird flu could risk pandemic ‘worse than Covid’ if it mutates
data.description:
The bird flu virus that has been spreading among wild birds, poultry and mammals could lead to a pandemic worse than Covid-19 if it mutates to transmit between humans, the head of France’s Institut Pasteur respiratory infections centre has said.
The highly pathogenic avian influenza, commonly called bird flu, has led to the culling of hundreds of millions of birds in the past few years, disrupting food supplies and driving up prices, though human infections remain rare.
“What we fear is the virus adapting to mammals, and particularly to humans, becoming capable of human-to-human transmission, and that virus would be a pandemic virus,” Marie-Anne Rameix-Welti, medical director at the Institut Pasteur’s respiratory infections centre, told Reuters.
The Institut Pasteur was among the first European labs to develop and share Covid-19 detection tests, making protocols available to the World Health Organization and labs worldwide.

People have antibodies against common H1 and H3 seasonal flu, but none against the H5 bird flu affecting birds and mammals, like they had none against Covid-19, she said.
And unlike Covid-19, which mainly affects vulnerable people, flu viruses can also kill healthy individuals, including children, Rameix-Welti said.
“A bird flu pandemic would probably be quite severe, potentially even more severe than the pandemic we experienced,” she said in her Paris laboratory.
There have been many cases of people infected by H5 bird flu viruses in the past, including the H5N1 currently circulating among poultry and dairy cows in the US, but these were often in close contact with infected animals.
A first ever human case of H5N5 appeared in the US state of Washington this month. The man, who had underlying conditions, died last week.
In its latest report on bird flu, the WHO said there had been nearly 1,000 outbreaks in humans between 2003 and 2025 - mainly in Egypt, Indonesia and Vietnam, of which 48 per cent had died.

However, the risk of a human pandemic developing remained low, Gregorio Torres, head of the Science Department at the World Organisation for Animal Health, said.
“We need to be prepared to respond early enough. But for the time being, you can happily walk in the forest, eat chicken and eggs and enjoy your life. The pandemic risk is a possibility. But in terms of probability, it’s still very low,” he said.
Rameix-Welti also said that if bird flu was to mutate to be able to be transmitted between humans, the world was better prepared than it was before the Covid-19 pandemic.
“The positive point with flu, compared to Covid, is we have specific preventive measures in place. We have vaccine candidates ready and know how to manufacture a vaccine quickly,” she said.
“We also have stocks of specific antivirals, that, in principle, would be effective against this avian influenza virus,” she added.

data.title: A new test for consent: reforming Hong Kong’s approach to sexual crimes
data.description:
Hong Kong has seen a number of amendments to its criminal law in recent years aimed at strengthening the response to sexual crimes.
Notable examples are the new offences in the Crimes Ordinance regarding voyeurism, upskirting and the publication of intimate images without consent.
There has also been discussion, including in this column, regarding how the law can adapt to the new phenomenon of pornography generated by artificial intelligence and deepfakes.
It is essential that the law, especially criminal law, be continuously updated in light of societal and technological developments. However, apart from looking forward, it is also essential in a rule of law society to look back on existing legislation and ask whether they remain fit for purpose.
Sexual offences are a key area that consider society’s evolving notions around sexual morality and issues of consent. The definition of rape in the Crimes Ordinance, for example, has remained on the books without significant amendment since its introduction in 1978.
The Hong Kong Law Reform Commission has been particularly active in this area, producing a consultation paper in 2012 on rape and other non-consensual sexual offences, as well as a report in 2019 reviewing all major substantive sexual offences in the city.
While the Law Reform Commission’s suggestions have yet to be implemented by the government, the chief executive in his recent policy address vowed to implement reforms to Hong Kong’s law on sexual offences. The Security Bureau is currently studying potential amendments to the law.
Reform in this area is to be welcomed, and it is worth discussing some broad areas that amendments to the law may consider.
The crime of rape is an excellent candidate for reform given its seriousness – it is punishable by up to life imprisonment – and the difficulty in successfully bringing cases to trial and securing convictions. Further legal clarity would not only assist with law enforcement but hopefully also provide the public with greater confidence and legal certainty.
According to statistics from the non-governmental organisation RainLily, between 2019 and 2023, only 47.2 per cent of victims that sought its help reported their “penetrative sexual assault” to police.
One of the main areas where the crime’s definition could be enhanced is the definition of consent, which is at the core of the gravamen of the offence. Sexual intercourse without the willing participation of the other party constitutes a grave infringement of bodily autonomy.
Whether the victim actually consented to the sexual act, therefore, is critical, as is the defendant’s mental state. At present, there is no statutory definition of consent, and it is left to the jury to decide in the circumstances, with directions from the judge, whether the victim consented to sexual intercourse.

There are a number of areas where the law can provide greater clarity as to the absence of consent, which would make it easier for judges to direct the jury and provide additional legal certainty.
One approach could be that taken by New Zealand’s Crimes Act 1961, which sets out a number of non-exhaustive circumstances in which a person permitting sexual activity does not equate to giving consent. Examples include situations in which force or the threat of force is applied.
Importantly, the New Zealand legislation explicitly states that a person should not be presumed to be consenting simply because they do not protest or offer physical resistance in response to sexual activity.
An added benefit of this approach is that its clear categories of conduct assist law enforcement with identifying potential cases of rape that could be investigated and reach the stage of a criminal trial.
There is also a deeper question as to how the question of consent is framed by the criminal law. At present, the statutory definition couches the crime in the absence of consent.
If an affirmative consent model is adopted, the initiator of sexual activity would have to be sure that the other party consented by taking steps to ascertain their consent. This would be coupled with provisions similar to those in New Zealand, where silence, inaction or lack of protest would be insufficient to presume consent.
This would in effect shift the focus of the trial from the acts of the victim (whether what they did can justify an inference that consent was absent) to the acts of the accused (whether they took steps to ascertain whether the other party consented).
Reform to this area of the law is much needed in order for Hong Kong’s sexual offences to reflect more nuanced understandings of consent. Of course, such reform must be undertaken carefully and with public consultation to ensure that the new offences are able to meet the needs of Hong Kong society and protect victims of sexual violence.
Martin Hui SC is the current vice-chairman of the Hong Kong Bar Association, a former deputy director of public prosecutions of Hong Kong and a criminal litigator.
Legal Tales is a new weekly column by senior members of the Hong Kong Bar Association presenting their perspectives on current affairs.

data.title: Soursweet, British drama on Hong Kong immigrants, showed grim reality of their new lives
data.description:
This is the latest instalment in a feature series reflecting on instances of East meets West in world cinema, including China-US co-productions.
Founded in 1982 as an offshoot of Channel 4, Film4 was committed to reinvigorating British cinema by platforming new voices and exploring complex, contemporary subjects. The 1988 immigration drama Soursweet is a prime example.
Based on the Booker Prize-shortlisted novel by British-Asian writer Timothy Mo, the film stars Taiwanese actress Sylvia Chang Ai-chia – who was soon to be a huge name in Asian cinema – as Lily, a Hong Kong woman who moves to London with her husband, Chen (Danny Dun An-ning), to start a new life.
Although well made and well intentioned, the film has the strange distinction of having almost no Chinese people behind the camera. Not that it stopped the careers of its key figures from soaring.


Director Mike Newell would go on to make the international smashes Four Weddings and a Funeral and Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire. Meanwhile, screenwriter Ian McEwan would prove himself one of the UK’s greatest novelists with Enduring Love, Amsterdam and Atonement, among others.
We first meet Lily and Chen on their wedding day in Hong Kong, where Chen announces that, under the British Nationality Act 1981, he has been invited to become a British citizen.
We quickly see the reality of their new life: a hot restaurant kitchen where Chen works as a waiter; a cramped flat that he, Lily and their son Man Kee (Speedy Choo) share with Lily’s sister Mui (Jodi Long); and dreams of escape.
“Chen, we are wasting life,” says the ambitious Lily. Her plan is for them to start their own takeaway, which they manage to do in a rundown part of South London. Asked if he has any concerns about his ability to cook, Chen remarks, “It’s takeaway food, the English like it. I don’t need to know anything about it!”
But when his triad connections catch up with them, it soon spells disaster.

If all this sounds a little unpromising, it is little wonder. Even for a writer of McEwan’s talents, adapting a 300-page, not-especially-cinematic novel for the screen is a thankless task.
Chen is an unappealing character, closed off and passive. Some of the secondary performances are awkward, not least because the characters speak in broken English rather than subtitled Cantonese. Furthermore, the scenes of triad violence feel beamed in from another film altogether.
But where Soursweet excels is in the intimacy with which it treats its main characters.
We watch Lily trying to build up Chen’s confidence while they are intimate. Meanwhile, the camera captures moments of quiet contemplation that speak volumes: a team of Chinese waiters eating together in silence after the hustle and bustle of service; Chen alone on the night bus, clutching his dinner in a plastic bag; or him swinging Man Kee in the grim park near the takeaway.

As played by the charismatic Chang, Lily is a force to be reckoned with. Whether scrubbing the restaurant floors to a shine in the early morning light or teaching her son to fight off bullies, she imbues the “tiger mum” archetype with real humanity.
“If someone hurts you, you never show it on your face,” she tells Man Kee.
The scene where Mui admits she is pregnant is particularly powerful, with Lily slapping her sister repeatedly in fury for letting them down. Mui, we understand, just wants to feel connected to something – anything – in this strange new place.
Later, in an especially moving sequence, we watch Mui give up her baby daughter for a better life – a poignant irony, given that this is what moving to England should have meant for them in the first place.

Like the title – which refers to the joys and hardships of the immigrant experience, as well as the most popular dish in the takeaway – it is a film of contrasts.
At work, Lily and her family experience extraordinary racism, but they are also racist themselves. Lily marvels at Caucasian faces – “All pink skin, like pigs” – and complains about the loose morals of the young women she serves.
In trying to confront racism, however, the film proves problematic itself. “Always knew you Chinese were sensible blokes, unlike these bloody West Indians,” their Greek neighbour, Constantinides, says.
Unfortunately, he is played by the Harrogate-born Jim Carter, adopting a comedically exaggerated accent.

In the context of the times, however, not to mention Film4’s remit, Soursweet was a step in the right direction. Admittedly, the credits read like a British public school register – full of Rogers, Richards and Adrians – and the credit “Special thanks to the Chinese community of Great Britain” feels patronising.
But there is something encouraging about British filmmakers at least trying to understand the lives of newcomers like Chen and Lily, even if it means that, ultimately, they still control the narrative.
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: Beijing freezes Japan youth exchange programmes over PM Takaichi’s Taiwan remarks
data.description:
Beijing has frozen youth exchange programmes with Tokyo amid continuing fallout over Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remarks on Taiwan, a diplomatic source said, raising concern that the bilateral rift could affect generations to come.
The Japanese side was told about the cancelled programmes in recent weeks after China launched retaliatory measures against Japan over Takaichi’s comments earlier this month, according to the diplomatic source.
“Usually, November and December are the season for youth exchanges, but now all are called off,” the source said.
Beijing has ramped up diplomatic and economic pressure on Japan, seeking to force Takaichi to retract her November 7 remarks suggesting Tokyo could deploy military forces in the event of a conflict in the Taiwan Strait. Takaichi has refused to retract them.


Citing safety reasons, China has asked its citizens to avoid travelling to Japan and told Chinese students to reconsider their plans to study there. The island nation is popular among Chinese students and has been one of the top destinations for Chinese tourists.
Dozens of concerts by Japanese artists, including veteran jazz musician Yoshio Suzuki and singer Kokia, were also cancelled. Kokia’s Beijing concert was cancelled at the last minute, though her shows in Guangzhou, Shenzhen and Chengdu went ahead last week.
In addition, Beijing postponed planned trilateral meetings with Japan and South Korea, including a leaders’ summit, saying Takaichi’s remarks had “damaged the foundation and atmosphere” of trilateral cooperation. Japan was slated to host this year’s summit.
Earlier this month, Japanese officials told Kyodo News that a group of 16 students and teachers from Wuxi in eastern China’s Jiangsu province had abruptly cancelled its trip to Sagamihara in Kanagawa prefecture. Wuxi and Sagamihara are sister cities.
Meanwhile, education officials in Okinawa prefecture said Chinese hosts had called off a planned visit by a group of 20 high school students originally scheduled to spend two weeks in Shanghai this Saturday for language and cultural exchange, Kyodo News reported.
No reason was given, though an Okinawan education official told Kyodo News that “the ongoing downturn in relations with China” could have been a factor.
The programme started in 2012 and has been held almost every year since then except during the coronavirus pandemic.
The moves could worsen public sentiment in China and Japan. Ties have frayed in recent years over the countries’ territorial disputes in the East China Sea, Tokyo’s alignment with Washington to counter Beijing and China’s larger military presence in the region.
For a long time, officials in China and Japan have hoped that a strong bond between their respective young people, especially through exchanges, could help stabilise relations that have often flared with tension over wartime history and territorial disputes.
During a high-level meeting in December last year, Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi and his then Japanese counterpart Takeshi Iwaya reached a 10-point agreement in which promoting youth exchange was among the highest priorities.
However, this year public sentiment in both countries has appeared to be suboptimal.

Between July and September, China released a number of big-budget films about Japan’s invasion of China and some of its most notorious atrocities as the mainland commemorated the 80th anniversary of Japan’s defeat in World War II.
This has fuelled unease about anti-Japanese sentiment in China, fresh from a series of attacks on Japanese citizens, including a fatal stabbing of a 10-year-old Japanese boy in the southern Chinese city of Shenzhen in September last year.
Shin Kawashima, an international relations professor at the University of Tokyo, called the halting of youth exchanges at this moment worrying.
Even though general public perception of China was negative in Japan, younger generations usually have held the most favourable views of China, Kawashima said.
“Under the current circumstances, halting youth exchanges is unlikely to improve Japan’s perception of China,” he added. “Maintaining such exchanges during periods of strained relations is, in fact, a more constructive approach.”
In a poll conducted by the Japanese government in October and November last year involving more than 3,000 adults, 31.3 per cent of respondents between the ages of 18 and 29 and 15.1 per cent between 30 and 39 said they had “friendly feelings” towards China.
While that was higher than other age groups, overall, only 14.7 per cent of all respondents said they had “friendly feelings”.
Kawashima said Beijing’s travel warning concerning Japan could be connected to its decision to freeze youth exchanges.
Beijing said the safety of Chinese citizens in Japan had “continued to deteriorate” because of rising crimes targeting Chinese nationals. Tokyo quickly rejected the claim, releasing crime data of cases involving Chinese in Japan and called Beijing’s assertion “incorrect”.
“Still, in theory, the Chinese government would not be able to send more young people to Japan” because of its travel warning, Kawashima added.
But the freeze order did not appear to affect other cultural exchange programmes or those involving university students.
Kyodo News reported that a delegation from Zhejiang University paid a courtesy visit to Masanao Shibahashi, the mayor of Gifu in central Japan, last Thursday. However, the meeting was changed from an open session to a closed-door one.
